card.indications: Treatment of hypertension and chronic stable or vasospastic angina. Extended-release formulations are preferred for chronic therapy.
card.mechanism: Blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and reducing systemic vascular resistance and myocardial oxygen demand.
card.dosing: Extended-release therapy commonly begins at 30 to 60 mg once daily and is titrated gradually. Immediate-release capsules should not be used for rapid blood-pressure lowering because of risk of severe hypotension and ischemic events.
card.routeAdministration: Give at the same time daily. Swallow extended-release tablets whole; do not crush, chew, or split unless the exact product permits. Monitor blood pressure and edema. Product shells may appear in stool.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Avoid use in cardiogenic shock and use caution in severe aortic stenosis, advanced heart failure, hepatic impairment, and marked hypotension.
card.warnings: Can cause excessive hypotension, reflex tachycardia, worsening angina or myocardial infarction during initiation or dose increase, peripheral edema, and rare gingival hyperplasia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, flushing, dizziness, peripheral edema, palpitations, nausea, and constipation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypotension, syncope, worsening angina, myocardial infarction, heart failure exacerbation, severe allergic reaction, and hepatic injury.
card.interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors can increase exposure and inducers can reduce it. Other antihypertensives and nitrates increase hypotension. Grapefruit may increase concentrations with some products.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, edema, weight, angina frequency, heart-failure symptoms, liver function when indicated, and dental or gingival changes with long-term therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Do not bite or puncture capsules for rapid effect. Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Rise slowly and report fainting, chest pain, marked swelling, or shortness of breath.
card.nutrition: Take consistently with or without food according to the exact product. Avoid grapefruit if instructed and follow prescribed sodium guidance.

card.indications: Treatment or prophylaxis of susceptible lower urinary-tract infections. It is not appropriate for pyelonephritis or perinephric abscess because tissue concentrations are inadequate.
card.mechanism: Bacterial enzymes reduce nitrofurantoin to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules.
card.dosing: Dose depends on formulation and indication. Common adult treatment regimens use 100 mg twice daily for selected formulations. Avoid use with significantly reduced renal function because efficacy falls and toxicity rises.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food to improve absorption and reduce GI upset. Shake suspension well and measure accurately. Complete the prescribed course. Verify renal function before therapy.
card.contraindications: Significant renal impairment, anuria or oliguria, pregnancy at term or during labor and delivery, neonates younger than 1 month, and prior nitrofurantoin-associated hepatic injury.
card.warnings: Can cause acute, subacute, or chronic pulmonary toxicity, hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, hemolytic anemia, and C. difficile diarrhea. Long-term prophylaxis requires close monitoring.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, decreased appetite, headache, diarrhea, and harmless brown discoloration of urine.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Pulmonary fibrosis or pneumonitis, hepatitis or hepatic failure, peripheral neuropathy, hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency, severe allergy, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: Magnesium-containing antacids may reduce absorption. Probenecid and sulfinpyrazone reduce renal excretion and may increase toxicity while lowering urinary concentrations.
card.monitoring: Monitor urinary symptoms and culture results, renal function, respiratory symptoms, liver tests with prolonged therapy, neurologic symptoms, CBC when indicated, and signs of hemolysis.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food and finish the course. Brown urine can occur. Report new cough, shortness of breath, numbness, tingling, jaundice, severe diarrhea, or persistent fever.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk and maintain hydration unless restricted. Avoid magnesium-containing antacids near the dose.

card.indications: Acute relief or prevention of angina, chronic angina prophylaxis with long-acting products, acute coronary syndromes, hypertensive emergencies, and acute heart failure in monitored settings.
card.mechanism: Converted to nitric oxide in vascular smooth muscle, increasing cyclic GMP and producing predominantly venous dilation, reduced preload, and decreased myocardial oxygen demand.
card.dosing: For acute angina, use one sublingual tablet or spray dose and repeat every 5 minutes for up to three doses while following emergency instructions. IV and long-acting dosing are titrated by indication and response. Include a daily nitrate-free interval with chronic products.
card.routeAdministration: Have the patient sit or lie down before sublingual dosing. Do not swallow sublingual tablets. Use glass or compatible tubing for IV infusion per protocol. Apply patches or ointment to clean, hairless skin and rotate sites; remove old product first.
card.contraindications: Concurrent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil, tadalafil, or vardenafil; riociguat; severe hypotension or shock; increased intracranial pressure; and severe anemia.
card.warnings: Can cause profound hypotension, syncope, reflex tachycardia, worsening ischemia, methemoglobinemia, and tolerance. PDE-5 inhibitor interaction may be fatal.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Throbbing headache, flushing, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, palpitations, and local skin irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypotension, syncope, myocardial ischemia, bradycardia, methemoglobinemia, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: PDE-5 inhibitors and riociguat are contraindicated. Alcohol, antihypertensives, opioids, and other vasodilators increase hypotension. Ergot derivatives may oppose the antianginal effect.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain response, blood pressure, pulse, ECG, oxygenation, headache, dizziness, IV site, nitrate tolerance, and methemoglobinemia if unexplained hypoxia occurs.
card.patientTeaching: At chest-pain onset, sit down, use one dose, and call emergency services according to the prescribed plan. Keep tablets in the original tightly closed container. Never combine with erectile-dysfunction drugs.
card.nutrition: Avoid alcohol because it worsens hypotension. Follow prescribed sodium and heart-healthy diet guidance.

card.indications: Restoration of blood pressure in severe acute hypotension, especially distributive or septic shock, after appropriate volume assessment and resuscitation.
card.mechanism: Primarily stimulates alpha1 receptors, causing intense vasoconstriction, with some beta1 activity that supports cardiac contractility.
card.dosing: Administer as a continuously titrated IV infusion according to hemodynamic targets and institutional protocol. Dose requirements vary widely. Correct hypovolemia when possible before initiation.
card.routeAdministration: Prefer central venous access, but a well-functioning proximal peripheral line may be used temporarily with frequent site checks. Use an infusion pump and continuous blood-pressure monitoring. Treat extravasation promptly with the approved antidote protocol.
card.contraindications: Uncorrected hypovolemia except as an emergency bridge and mesenteric or peripheral vascular thrombosis when further ischemia is likely. Risk-benefit may override contraindications in life-threatening shock.
card.warnings: Can cause severe hypertension, arrhythmias, tissue ischemia, limb or mesenteric necrosis, and extravasation injury. Abrupt discontinuation may cause profound hypotension.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, anxiety, tremor, palpitations, hypertension, bradycardia, decreased peripheral perfusion, and infusion-site irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Ventricular arrhythmias, myocardial ischemia, stroke, pulmonary edema, peripheral or mesenteric ischemia, tissue necrosis, and severe extravasation injury.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors, tricyclic antidepressants, linezolid, stimulants, and other sympathomimetics can intensify pressor effects. Halogenated anesthetics may increase arrhythmias.
card.monitoring: Continuously monitor arterial or frequent noninvasive blood pressure, ECG, heart rate, urine output, lactate, mental status, peripheral perfusion, extremity temperature and color, and IV site.
card.patientTeaching: Explain the need for continuous monitoring and restricted movement around the infusion line. Report burning, pain, coldness, numbness, or swelling at the IV site immediately.
card.nutrition: Nutrition is secondary during unstable shock. Manage fluids and enteral intake according to perfusion status and critical-care orders.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible Candida infections of the mouth, GI tract, skin, and vagina. It is not absorbed sufficiently for systemic fungal infections.
card.mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, forming pores that cause leakage of intracellular contents and fungal cell death.
card.dosing: Dose and frequency vary by site and formulation. Oral suspension for thrush is commonly given several times daily and retained in the mouth before swallowing when appropriate. Continue for the prescribed duration after symptoms improve.
card.routeAdministration: Shake oral suspension well. Distribute throughout the mouth and retain as long as possible before swallowing. Apply topical products to clean, dry skin. Avoid occlusive dressings unless ordered.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to nystatin or product components.
card.warnings: Local irritation and hypersensitivity may occur. Oral products are not effective for systemic candidiasis. Some formulations contain significant sugar or alcohol.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Mouth irritation, unpleasant taste, nausea, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, and local burning, itching, or rash.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypersensitivity, facial swelling, bronchospasm, and severe skin reactions are rare.
card.interactions: Clinically important systemic interactions are minimal because absorption is poor. Local products may be affected by concurrent topical agents.
card.monitoring: Monitor lesion appearance, oral pain, swallowing, skin integrity, symptom resolution, recurrence, and signs of systemic infection that require different therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Use for the full prescribed duration. For oral thrush, swish thoroughly and avoid eating or drinking briefly after the dose. Clean dentures, pacifiers, or feeding equipment as directed to prevent reinfection.
card.nutrition: Avoid food and drink for about 30 minutes after oral suspension when possible. Maintain oral hygiene and manage diabetes or high sugar intake that may promote Candida growth.
